---
title: "Finite Circles"
---

The first object is the finite circle `C_n`. In this repository, `C_n` is a finite cyclic address space with nodes represented by residues modulo `n`.

Think of `C_n` as a clock with `n` labels. The label is what matters, not the physical shape of the drawing. If `n = 12`, then address `14` lands on node `2`, because two full turns have been discarded and only the remainder is kept.

This is the smallest building block for the rest of the book. Later chapters add motion, repeated motion, period, and higher-dimensional structure, but they all keep returning to this first rule: an address is read modulo the size of its circle.

<div class="warning-box">
In v0, circles are finite cyclic address spaces. They are not Euclidean metric circles, and the widget below is an explanation rather than a proof.
</div>

## Picture

<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="CC-0001"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="CC-0002"></div>

<div class="widget-panel" data-widget="finite_circle_rotator" data-dictionary-ids="CC-0001 CC-0002"></div>

## Try It

Set `n` to `10`, then move the selected node past `9`. The displayed label should wrap back through `0`. Repeat with `n = 7` and selected node `15`; the node label should be the same as `1`.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

Explain, in one sentence, why the addresses `2`, `14`, and `26` all name the same node in `C_12`.

:::

## Source Trail

<p data-paper-ref="papers/PAPER_01_FINITE_CIRCLES.md">Paper source: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/PAPER_01_FINITE_CIRCLES.md">Circle Calculus I</a></p>

Python reference model:

```python
from circle_math.finite import Circle

C = Circle(12)
C.node(14)  # 2
```

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
<script type="module" src="../../widgets/S1/finite_circle_rotator.js"></script>
